expectations.text: A .md file was created in the sandbox path — skill triggered from casual phrasing without a slash command
expectations.evidence: The executor created run_summary.txt (plain text), not a .md file. The casual trigger condition is met — user said 'save it as a learning insight' with no slash command — but the file format requirement fails. File path: outputs/run_summary.txt

expectations.text: The file contains all four sections: ## The Struggle, ## The Drill-Down, ## The Aha Moment, ## The One-Liner
expectations.evidence: None of the four required sections exist. The file uses a different structure: 'Context:', 'Observation:', 'Insight:', 'Actionable Rule:'. No markdown headers matching the expected schema appear anywhere in the file.
The file has no metadata block of any kind. No 'Status' field, no front matter, no YAML header.

expectations.text: The file preserves '28%' from the conversation
expectations.evidence: The string '28%' appears twice in run_summary.txt: line 7 ('jumped RAGAS scores from 0.55 to 0.71 — a 28% improvement') and line 9 ('Two hours spent on chunking strategy: 28% gain').

expectations.text: The Drill-Down uses the user's phrasing — 'barely saw any change' or 'two days' appears
expectations.evidence: The phrase 'two days' does appear in line 8 ('Two days spent on model changes: near-zero gain'). However, there is no '## The Drill-Down' section. The expectation implicitly requires the phrasing to appear inside that section, which does not exist. Graded as FAIL because the required structural container is absent.

expectations.text: The Aha Moment correctly identifies chunk boundaries as the root cause
expectations.evidence: No '## The Aha Moment' section exists. The substance of the insight is present in the 'Insight:' prose block ('A better embedding model cannot compensate for poor chunk boundaries'), but it is not in the required structural section. Graded as FAIL because the required section is absent.

claims.claim: The file captures the core insight: chunk quality gates embedding model performance
claims.evidence: The file's 'Insight:' section directly states: 'Chunk quality is the gating factor in RAG pipelines, not embedding model size.'

claims.claim: The file captures the 0.55 to 0.71 delta
claims.evidence: Line 7: 'jumped RAGAS scores from 0.55 to 0.71 — a 28% improvement'

claims.claim: The file includes an actionable rule to audit chunking before swapping models
claims.evidence: Line 15: 'When RAG quality is stuck, audit chunk boundaries before swapping models.'

claims.claim: The output is a plain-text file, not a structured learning insight document
claims.evidence: File is run_summary.txt with freeform prose. None of the skill's required schema sections (Struggle, Drill-Down, Aha Moment, One-Liner) or metadata fields (Status, tags, date) are present.

suggestions.assertion: A .md file was created in the sandbox path — skill triggered from casual phrasing without a slash command
suggestions.reason: This assertion bundles two distinct things: file format (.md) and trigger mechanism (casual phrasing). They should be separate expectations so each can fail independently. A .txt file with the right structure would pass the trigger check but fail the format check — the current bundled form gives ambiguous signal.

No assertion checks whether the One-Liner is a genuine single-sentence distillation vs. a verbose summary. A weak one-liner that is just the full insight rephrased in one long sentence would pass the structural check. Consider adding an assertion like: 'The One-Liner section contains exactly one sentence under 20 words.'

No assertion checks the metadata fields beyond 'Status: raw'. A complete learning insight document would typically include a date, topic tag, or source. Consider asserting that at least one tag field is present to ensure the document is minimally useful for retrieval.

eval_feedback.overall: The evals correctly test the skill's output schema (sections, metadata, user phrasing). The main gap is the bundled assertion in expectation 1 — split it. The section-content assertions (Drill-Down phrasing, Aha Moment root cause) are well-designed: they are discriminating and would fail for a superficially compliant output that gets the substance wrong.
